step_description: Memory grant usage — queries waiting and holding large grants
rows: SELECT r.region, p.category, SUM(oi.qty * p.price) AS revenue FROM OrderItems oi JOIN Products p ON oi.product_id = p.id JOIN Orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id JOIN Regions r ON o.region_id = r.id GROUP BY r.region, p.category ORDER BY revenue DESC

SELECT r.region, p.category, SUM(oi.qty * p.price) AS revenue FROM OrderItems oi JOIN Products p ON oi.product_id = p.id JOIN Orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id JOIN Regions r ON o.region_id = r.id GROUP BY r.region, p.category ORDER BY revenue DESC

SELECT r.region, p.category, SUM(oi.qty * p.price) AS revenue FROM OrderItems oi JOIN Products p ON oi.product_id = p.id JOIN Orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id JOIN Regions r ON o.region_id = r.id GROUP BY r.region, p.category ORDER BY revenue DESC
SELECT TOP 100 o.order_id, o.total FROM Orders o WHERE o.region_id = @rid ORDER BY o.created_date DESC
narrative_hint: One query is the source of the entire RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE crisis. The revenue report query is requesting 28,420 MB (28 GB) of memory per execution — 33x its ideal grant of 840MB. Session 284 holds the grant and is executing. Sessions 301 and 312 are queued and waiting 84-92 seconds for their grants. Three concurrent executions of this query would require 84GB of memory grants, exhausting the server's max server memory. The query_cost_estimate of 48,420 means the optimizer believes this is an extremely expensive operation — likely because an index rebuild changed the statistics to show much larger row counts for the joins. The DOP of 8 multiplies the memory request 8x. Claude should identify this query as the culprit and proceed to Query Store to find the plan change.
